Original reason deprioritized: Investigation paused — skepticism about cost estimate and modeling complexity
Reason: WHO published first formal clinical guideline on RF/RHD in Nov 2024 (GRADE methodology; strong recommendation for empirical antibiotic treatment in endemic LMIC settings). GBD 2021 shows India burden ~29% higher than assumed (166k vs 129k deaths). 2023 Lancet India CEA finds secondary prevention cost-effective at ~$30/QALY. BOTEC includes 3x cost multiplier and benefit-stream adjustments. Cost remains the pivotal unknown. See writeup.

Intervention name: Uterotonics for prevention of postpartum hemorrhage
Original CE estimate (x cash): Not calculated (BOTEC incomplete; naive misoprostol estimate ~$700/life not credible — no mortality evidence)
Original reason deprioritized: No mortality evidence for misoprostol; TXA trial results not yet published at time of writing
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~3x (best guess; range 2-7x depending on program costs)
Reason: Rebuilt BOTEC models bundled HSC+TXA program at $2/birth. TXA now WHO-recommended for treatment (not prevention) with confirmed effect (WOMAN trial RR 0.81; 2024 Lancet meta-analysis OR 0.77). HSC is new WHO-recommended product ($0.31/ampoule). CE highly sensitive to per-birth cost — at $1/birth reaches ~6.5x. AMPLI-PPHI cost data (expected mid-2026) would resolve key uncertainty. See writeup.

Original CE estimate (x cash): ~$3777/death averted (Ghana iCCM proxy — multiples of cash not computed but likely ~5–7x based on moral weights)
Original reason deprioritized: Not cost-effective enough (likely near but below 8x bar); conflicting evidence (Ghana vs. Burkina Faso RCTs)
Reason: ProCCM RCT (Mali 2017-2020) found no mortality benefit from proactive home visits specifically vs. control arm (both arms received professional CHWs + reinforced clinics). Muso Health observational data (2008-2015) shows dramatic U5MR decline (154→7/1000) at ~$8/person/year but no control group. GiveWell has independently reviewed iCCM and deprioritized it. No new evidence materially raises CE estimate.

Original reason deprioritized: Weak evidence quality (Cochrane 2020: insufficient evidence to compare Ponseti to traditional techniques); high uncertainty about long-term effectiveness (~47% relapse rate at 10 years)
Reason: GiveWell already investigated in depth and awarded a $5.2M grant to MiracleFeet in Jan 2023, estimating CE at ~8x (slightly below 10x bar). Cost per case ($530) is higher than the 2022 QEA assumed ($414). Evidence quality has not improved — still no RCTs of Ponseti vs. untreated controls. 2024 systematic review confirms wide success range (55–100%) and relapse range (3–34%). M&E study underway (GiveWell $600k grant; results expected ~2027) is the next decision-relevant inflection point. No new WHO guidelines on clubfoot.

Original reason deprioritized: Not cost-effective (CE an order of magnitude below bar); non-significant RCT result (RR 0.88 p=0.36); uncertain DALY burden from preventing diabetes short-term
Reason: No material developments since 2022. The K-DPP RCT (2018) remains the sole trial; its 12% diabetes reduction is non-significant (p=0.36). A 9-year follow-up study is underway (protocol published March 2023) but results are not yet published. GBD 2021 confirms growing diabetes burden in LMICs (DALYs up ~42%) but this does not help CE arithmetic — the intervention effect is too small and the DALY-per-case-prevented estimate (speculatively 1) too uncertain. Even at 10 DALYs per case prevented the CE would only reach ~6x. The gap to 8x is too large to bridge with plausible parameter changes.

Original reason deprioritized: Not cost-effective as standalone program; CE highly dependent on delivery platform cost; limited RFMF (~$32M one-time in India)
Reason: Standalone CE (3.7x) far below bar. Platform delivery (PxD) threshold analysis (9.9x) barely clears 8x and depends on PxD cost structure. Some favorable updates: (1) no yield penalty in non-flood years confirmed (removes a key downside risk); (2) PxD actively distributing Swarna-Sub1 in West Bengal; (3) climate change increasing flood frequency. But GiveWell is already investigating PxD separately (scoping grant $540k March 2022) — flood-tolerant rice bundling would be part of that broader evaluation. Adoption still low (~1% in Bangladesh per DNA fingerprinting). Next-gen varieties surviving 3+ weeks submergence in development but not yet deployed. Not worth independent investigation.

Original CE estimate (x cash): ~13x median across 5 countries (range 5–38x; India outlier at 38x)
Original reason deprioritized: Speculative — CE model borrowed from alcohol policy BOTEC with minimal adaptation; uncertain enforcement costs; Bloomberg already heavily funding road safety
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~8–12x (likely lower than original due to reduced neglectedness)
Reason: Space has become less neglected since 2022: Bloomberg doubled commitment to $240M (2020-2025) covering 16 countries including Brazil/Egypt/Ethiopia/India — the same countries modeled in the QEA. Original CE highly speculative (parameters borrowed from alcohol policy model; key inputs like 35% advocacy success rate and 4% burden reduction are guesses). Government enforcement costs were likely underestimated (-20% adjustment; CE report notes enforcement can increase $/DALY up to 10x). India (38x) drives most of CE but Bloomberg already active there (Mumbai/Delhi/Bengaluru). Rethink Priorities found some niche remaining opportunities (Pakistan/Thailand) but overall space is well-funded. GBD 2021: 1.2M deaths/65M DALYs unchanged. Not worth independent investigation — limited counterfactual impact given Bloomberg dominance.

Original reason deprioritized: Pending trial results — original was threshold analysis only; flagged for revisit in early 2023
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~4x at $3/pregnancy in Nigeria (range ~2x at $7 to ~9x at $1.50; highly cost-sensitive)
Reason: A-PLUS trial (NEJM 2023 n=29278) found strong maternal benefit (RR 0.67 for maternal sepsis/death; 95% CI 0.56-0.79) but null neonatal result (RR 1.02). PregnAnZI-2 confirmed neonatal null. Neonatal mortality drove 66-86% of original CE — now zero. Maternal effect is 4x larger than assumed (33% vs 8%) but applies to relatively few deaths per pregnancy. CE in high-MMR settings (Nigeria 735/100k) could reach 8x+ at low delivery costs ($1.50/pregnancy). Drug costs only $0.91/dose. Lancet Global Health 2025 CEA finds cost-saving from health system perspective. No WHO recommendation yet; AMR concerns remain. See writeup.

Intervention name: Phone audits to improve government service delivery
Original reason deprioritized: Not cost-effective — underlying cash transfer program in middle-income India is far less CE than GiveDirectly; leverage framing yields negative CE by standard assumptions; no identifiable implementer for TA
Reason: No material developments since 2022. The original RCT (Muralidharan et al. 2021; published AEJ:Applied 2021) found a 1.34pp increase in check encashment (p=0.037) at $0.036 per additional dollar delivered. However CE is 0.15x because the underlying program (cash transfers in Telangana; per capita income $2817) is not cost-effective by GW standards. The research team has expanded to Pakistan and Paraguay via J-PAL but no new published results. No organization identified to provide TA for this approach. The concept of phone monitoring for GW-relevant programs (e.g. health service delivery in SSA) is interesting in principle but entirely speculative — no evidence on health outcomes and no concrete pathway to a fundable opportunity.

Intervention name: CCTs for antenatal care facility delivery and postnatal care
Original reason deprioritized: Below 8x bar; based on single Nigerian RCT (Okeke & Abubakar 2020); highly uncertain costs and program design; no clear implementer at scale
Reason: No material developments since 2022. The sole RCT with mortality outcomes (Okeke & Abubakar 2020 Nigeria n=10852) found a 29% reduction in miscarriages and 23% reduction in stillbirths from a $14 CCT. But CE at 4x (full package) or 7x (ANC-only) is below the 8x bar. Costs are highly uncertain ($85/woman for full package including admin costs). The Afya trial in Kenya (2022 results) found CCTs for maternal health were not cost-effective (INT$1035 per additional ANC visit). No new RCTs with mortality outcomes since the original. New Incentives demonstrates CCTs work for immunization (9-38x) but that is a different mechanism. GBD 2021 confirms SSA stillbirth burden remains very high (77% of global total) but this does not help CE arithmetic. Even ANC-only at 7x does not clear bar and requires strong assumptions about neonatal mortality reduction from observational data.

Original CE estimate (x cash): ~28x (crude BOTEC using HIC effect size with no IV/EV adjustment)
Original reason deprioritized: Postpone — only LMIC trial (ACT 2014) showed harm from overtreatment; flagged for revisit when follow-up trials report
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~10–20x (highly dependent on program implementation costs; range ~6x at $100/woman to ~39x at $10/woman)
Reason: The evidence has shifted dramatically since the original QEA. The WHO ACTION-I trial (NEJM 2020 n=2852 across Bangladesh/India/Kenya/Nigeria/Pakistan) found ACS reduced neonatal death by 16% (RR 0.84; 95% CI 0.72-0.97) in properly targeted hospital settings — resolving the key concern from the ACT trial (which showed harm due to overtreatment of non-preterm births). WHO updated ACS guidelines in 2022 based on ACTION-I. Cost-effectiveness analysis found ACS cost-saving in all 5 trial countries. But implementation gap is massive (~10% of LMIC facilities provide ACS). No clear philanthropic entry point — scale-up requires ultrasound dating capacity + trained staff + adequate neonatal care. Program-level costs are the pivotal unknown. See writeup.

Original CE estimate (x cash): ~9x (average over 13 years; range 1x in Phase I to 11x in Phase III)
Original reason deprioritized: Back-loaded CE (Phase I ~3x); long timeframe (13 years for DRC); no RCTs — evidence entirely from models and retrospective evaluations; high implementation risk
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~9–14x (improved by GAVI PEP support but still back-loaded and uncertain)
Reason: Original CE model was well-constructed but based entirely on hypothetical 13-year DRC program with no experimental evidence. Major update: GAVI announced support for human rabies PEP in 50+ countries (June 2024) — reduces early-year program costs by ~60% since PEP was 60% of Phase I costs. This could raise average CE from ~9x to ~14x. 2023 Nature Communications analysis confirms large welfare gains ($9.5B over 30 years) from coordinated African vaccination. But: (1) still no RCTs linking MDV to human mortality; (2) 13-year sustained commitment required; (3) Phase I CE still marginal even with GAVI (~3–5x); (4) Zero by 30 progress is slow; (5) RFMF has decreased with GAVI entry and GARC/WHO coordination. Per-dog vaccination costs may be lower than assumed (~$2.70-2.85 vs $4.43 in BOTEC). Not worth independent investigation given lack of experimental evidence and reduced neglectedness.

Original CE estimate (x cash): Not available (original QEA/BOTEC inaccessible due to auth issue)
Original reason deprioritized: Unclear from original — likely flagged due to weak evidence base and implementation challenges
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): Not cost-effective (no evidence of health benefit from RCTs)
Reason: Two large RCTs have now definitively shown that LPG cookstove interventions do not reduce child health outcomes. The HAPIN trial (NEJM 2024 n=3200 across Guatemala/India/Peru/Rwanda) found no effect on severe infant pneumonia birthweight or stunting. The GRAPHS trial (Ghana) similarly found no significant effect on birthweight or pneumonia. GiveWell has independently investigated clean cookstoves and concluded it is not a priority program due to lack of clear evidence of effectiveness at reducing health problems. Key implementation barriers include low adoption rates inadequate air quality improvements (PM2.5 still far above WHO standards even with LPG) and low sustained usage. There is no plausible pathway to cost-effective health impact from this intervention.

Intervention name: Selenium supplementation for neonatal sepsis prevention
Original reason deprioritized: Insufficient evidence — only one small LMIC trial (Aggarwal 2016 n=90 India); waiting for Gates-funded research
Reason: No new LMIC trials since Aggarwal 2016 (n=90). The Cochrane review (Darlow 2003 updated) still covers only 3 HIC/LMIC studies. Pooled RR for sepsis episodes is 0.73 (95% CI 0.57-0.93) but not powered for mortality. No WHO recommendation for neonatal selenium supplementation. The Gates-funded research mentioned in the original has not materialized into published results. A 2024 observational study (PMC11626566) confirms association between selenium deficiency and neonatal sepsis but adds no causal evidence. CE at pooled estimate ($3/infant): ~5x. At Aggarwal-only ($1/infant): ~25x but based on n=90 with acknowledged upward biases (excluded early-onset sepsis; used 'sepsis and other infections' from GBD). Evidence base remains too thin to act on.

Original CE estimate (x cash): 2.7–5.3x (across 5 West African expansion countries after IV/EV and leverage adjustments)
Original reason deprioritized: Below 8x bar; one RCT (Glennerster et al 2021 Burkina Faso); uncertain duration of contraceptive use; low moral weight for CYP (0.67 UoV)
Reason: CE across West Africa expansion countries: 2.7x (Sierra Leone) to 5.3x (Niger) after 50% IV and 75% EV adjustments plus leverage/funging. Even with optimistic 2-year duration assumption (vs 1.5 years), best case is ~7.1x in Niger — still below 8x bar. GiveWell funded a $500k grant to Family Empowerment Media (FEM) for an RCT in Nigeria (March 2023); trial started mid-2024 but no results yet. DMI scaled to 39 radio stations in Burkina Faso reaching 16.3M people. No new RCTs with results since Glennerster et al 2021. CYP moral weight (0.67 UoV) would need to increase >50% AND cost assumptions would need to improve for CE to approach 8x. FEM RCT results (expected ~2026-2027) could be decision-relevant.

Original reason deprioritized: Low CE due to high cost ($540/person) and moderate morbidity/consumption effects; GW values mortality prevention more than YLD aversion
Reason: No new RCTs or quasi-experiments since Wydick et al 2020 (n=1118 India). CE at 3x (general) remains well below 8x bar. Severe-only targeting (9x) still faces ethical/reputational barriers — NGOs like Operation Smile/Smile Train unlikely to exclude non-severe cases. A 2023 systematic review (Chung et al) found methodology limitations in existing CLP cost-effectiveness analyses (disability weights skewed to HIC; exclude malnutrition and social stigma). Rural Kenya data suggests surgical costs as low as $201 (vs $540), but this is facility cost only and may not reflect total program costs. GiveWell has not revisited CLP surgery since the 2021 QEA. ~93% of value from YLDs averted — fundamentally limited by GW's relative valuation of morbidity vs mortality.

Original reason deprioritized: Uncertain RFMF; rough CEA with key cost exclusions; evidence limited to two Kenya RCTs of self-tests for male partners of pregnant women
Reason: The intervention itself is effective and WHO-recommended (since 2016; updated 2022-23 to include PrEP initiation). Two Kenya RCTs confirmed HIVST roughly doubles testing uptake among male partners. CEA studies in West Africa (ATLAS programme) find $27-126/DALY averted — highly cost-effective. But the space is no longer neglected: PEPFAR Global Fund and UNITAID have massively funded HIVST scale-up through the STAR initiative (2016-2020 6 countries) and beyond. Multiple countries now have national HIVST policies. The original BOTEC's CE of 10x explicitly excluded ART costs ($100-200+/person/year for 18+ years). With ART costs included (even via leverage/funging) CE would be substantially lower. GiveWell's comparative advantage in funding HIVST is limited given massive existing funding flows. Cost per kit distributed: $9-50 depending on population and country.

Original CE estimate (x cash): ~15x at $8/woman (made-up costs); ~5x at CHAI costs + GBD mortality
Original reason deprioritized: Weak evidence (1 underpowered RCT + 5 observational studies); cost per woman highly uncertain; awaiting CHAI cost data
Reason: Pooled non-experimental evidence: RR 0.52 (95% CI 0.36-0.77) for maternal mortality from obstetric hemorrhage. One underpowered cluster-RCT (Miller et al 2013 n=887 15 deaths) found non-significant OR 0.36. Evidence hasn't changed since the original (no new RCTs and authors say an adequately powered RCT is infeasible). However CHAI Zimbabwe pilot (2019-2021) showed real-world promise: NASG used in 90% of potential applications across 34 facilities 50% fewer maternal deaths and zero PPH deaths. Zimbabwe MOHCC has adopted NASG for national rollout. Zambia also scaling (2023 process evaluation published). Device is simple ($75 reusable 72+ times) and WHO-recommended (2012 weak recommendation). Key uncertainties remain: cost per woman ($8 was a guess; CHAI Zimbabwe suggests $750k for national 5-year scale-up). Complements E-MOTIVE PPH bundle (which showed RR 0.40 for severe PPH outcomes NEJM 2023). See writeup.

Original reason deprioritized: High uncertainty about long-term consumption estimation from test scores; GW has no consensus methodology for education-to-consumption pathway
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~5–10x (scale-up shows lower effects but also lower costs)
Reason: Original CE ranged from 2.2x (Brown methodology) to 9.1x (Evans & Yuan) with potential up to 32x. Scale-up in Rajasthan (J-PAL Year 2 RCT 2019) found somewhat lower effects (0.2 SD in math/Hindi vs 0.37/0.23 in original). However at-scale costs may be dramatically lower: Rs. 130/child/year/subject (~$1.50/year) vs $4/month ($48/year) in original. Net effect on CE is ambiguous. Fundamental issue remains: GiveWell has no consensus methodology for estimating consumption gains from test score improvements. The BOTEC is entirely driven by this uncertain conversion. Cognitive fade-out effects are not modeled. Mindspark operates only in India (15 government schools 6000 students as of 2024-25). No clear pathway to GW funding — education is not GW's primary domain and India is not a priority context. Would require GW to resolve its consumption estimation methodology before this could move forward.

Original reason deprioritized: Waiting for Bloomberg/Johns Hopkins study results; very brief QEA (~45 minutes)
Reason: The Bloomberg/Johns Hopkins PRECISE study has now been published. The crèche intervention in rural Bangladesh reduced drowning by 89% (higher than the preliminary 70% claim). Cost per child: $16/year — well above the $2.50/year breakeven for GW CE. ICER: $17,008/life saved or $362/DALY averted. By GW standards ($5-8k/life saved at 8x bar) this is ~0.5x cash — far below threshold. Bangladesh government approved $32M 3-year program in February 2022 scaling from 2,500 to 8,000 childcare sites for 200,000 children — reducing neglectedness further. 2024 global scoping review (Frontiers in Public Health) confirms effectiveness of supervision-based interventions but does not identify cheaper delivery models. Secondary benefits (injury prevention cognitive development) exist but are not enough to bridge the CE gap.

Intervention name: Rape/sexual violence prevention (No Means No Worldwide)
Original CE estimate (x cash): No CE model produced (moral weight for rape prevention unresolved)
Original reason deprioritized: Methodological weaknesses in two RCTs (experimenter demand effects school-level analysis not pre-registered); awaiting third RCT; no cost-effectiveness model
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): Not calculable (moral weight issue persists; newer RCT null)
Reason: A newer cluster-RCT of the IMpower + SOS program in Nairobi informal settlements (2016-2018 published Prevention Science 2023) found NO significant effect on sexual assault (OR 1.21; 95% CI 0.40-5.21 p=0.63). This contradicts the earlier Baiocchi 2016 result (51% decrease) and partially contradicts Decker 2018 (Malawi RR 0.68). The null result validates the methodological concerns flagged in the original QEA — earlier positive findings may have been driven by experimenter demand effects (instructors conducted surveys) and school-level analysis artifacts. GiveWell still has no moral weight for rape/sexual assault prevention making CE comparison to cash transfers impossible. Even if effects were real the program would require GW to establish how to value preventing sexual violence relative to preventing death — a sensitive methodological question the original QEA explicitly deferred. Not worth revisiting given the null third RCT.

Intervention name: Antibiotic treatment of bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy
Original CE estimate (x cash): No BOTEC (antibiotics clear BV but don't prevent preterm birth)
Original reason deprioritized: Strong BV-PTB association but mass antibiotic treatment doesn't reduce preterm birth (Cochrane RR 0.88 NS); mechanism unclear
Reason: Two major new publications confirm the original finding. (1) AuTop RCT (JAMA 2023 n=6671 Australia): molecular screening + treatment for BV before 20 weeks found no significant reduction in preterm birth (3.8% vs 4.6% p=NS). (2) Klebanoff 2023 individual participant data meta-analysis: antibiotic treatment of BV does not reduce preterm delivery. A 2025 network meta-analysis found probiotics had the most potential but evidence remains preliminary. One positive signal: a 2024 systematic review found BV treatment may reduce PTB specifically in high-risk women — but 'high-risk' screening is difficult to operationalize in LMIC settings and the effect is from a subgroup analysis. The fundamental puzzle from the original QEA persists: BV is strongly associated with PTB but treating it doesn't prevent PTB. The original speculation (BV may be a marker for another condition or the resistant infections are the ones causing PTB) remains unresolved. No pathway to GW-fundable intervention.

Original CE estimate (x cash): ~3–4x (writeup rough estimate; BOTEC gives ~0.5x over 10 years)
Original reason deprioritized: No direct evidence on program effectiveness (only controlled research plots); cost-effectiveness uncertain; agriculture/income pathway not GW's core focus
Reason: An agriculture/income intervention to control parasitic striga weed on maize in SSA. The IITA ISMA evaluation (Nigeria) showed 55% adoption and 46.8% higher income for participants but this is non-randomized (selection bias likely). Push-pull technology has scaled to ~350k farmers across 18 countries by 2024 (icipe) but remains tiny vs ~100M affected. Striga-resistant maize breeding has progressed (3.4-3.7% annual yield gains in trials) but farm-level adoption remains low. Still no RCTs on program-level effectiveness. Even optimistic 10-year benefit assumptions yield CE well below 8x: the $36/farmer cost and $22.76/year yield gain translate to ~0.5x GD in the BOTEC. The writeup's 3-4x claim was a rough judgment call not derivable from the BOTEC. Fundamentally limited by the income-to-UoV conversion — no mortality channel. Not worth revisiting.

Intervention name: Delivering a pulse oximeter + safe surgery checklist training
Original reason deprioritized: No RCT evidence for mortality reduction from pulse oximeters (Cochrane review); safe surgery checklist evidence weak (before-and-after studies; Ontario natural experiment found no effect)
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): Possibly favorable ($115/DALY) but based on unverified 10% mortality assumption
Reason: The fundamental evidence gap from the 2017 QEA has not been closed. Cochrane review finding (no evidence pulse oximeters reduce mortality/morbidity in surgery) remains unchanged. WHO Safe Surgery Checklist evidence has improved somewhat: a stepped-wedge cluster RCT found reduced complications and hospital stay; South Carolina DiD analysis (3 years) found 22% mortality reduction; meta-analyses report 44% complication reduction and 23% mortality decrease. But no LMIC RCT on mortality for either intervention. The Lancet Global Health Oxygen Commission (2025) found child deaths fell 25% with pulse oximetry + oxygen but this is for pneumonia management broadly — not surgical pulse oximetry specifically. A 2014 cost-effectiveness analysis found $115/DALY averted for hand-held oximeters but this assumed 10% of anaesthesia-related deaths prevented — an assumption not backed by experimental evidence. Lifebox has distributed 35k oximeters since 2011. Strong expert consensus supports the intervention but GW's framework requires stronger causal evidence. Surgical safety is not GW's typical domain. Not worth revisiting.

Original CE estimate (x cash): ~$88700/death averted (mortality channel); main benefit was avoided unwanted pregnancies (~$269 each) and avoided unsafe abortion complications (~$1224 each)
Original reason deprioritized: Mortality channel weak; CE depended on unresolved moral weight for avoided unwanted pregnancies; self-injection not yet approved
Reason: GiveWell published a contraception valuation framework (April 2025): 0.7 UoV per woman-year of modern contraception (range 0.3-1.1). This directly resolves the moral weight blocker from the 2016 QEA. Self-injection is now approved in 35+ countries and available in 55+ countries. Meta-analysis of 3 RCTs: self-injection increases 12-month continuation by 27% (RR 1.27 95% CI 1.16-1.39). Uganda cohort: 81% vs 65% at 12 months. Phase 3 trial confirms effectiveness at 4-month intervals (99% efficacy). Using GW's valuation at $12/year (commodity $4 + delivery $8): ~13x. Even at $20/year: ~7x — near bar. At $6/year (lean self-injection): ~35x. Cost per woman-year is the pivotal unknown. GW is actively investigating family planning (FEM grant $500k podcast episode on family planning). Commodity cost locked at $1/dose via Pfizer/Gates/CIFF agreement. See writeup.

Original reason deprioritized: Uncertain additionality (do programs replace unsafe abortions or displace other safe options?); unresolved GW moral weight for safe abortion; low mortality case fatality rate (~0.3-0.5%)
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~3–10x (evidence base strengthened but GW-internal blockers unchanged)
Reason: Evidence base for medical abortion has strengthened materially. WHO 2022 guidelines now recommend self-management of medical abortion and telemedicine delivery for the first time — a significant shift that addresses the original concern about women not having access to health facilities. Multiple LMIC studies show 89-95% complete abortion with self-managed mifepristone+misoprostol. PSI reached ~859k women in 2018. Drug quality is a concern (55% of LMIC samples non-compliant in multi-country study). But the GW-specific blockers from the original QEA have not changed: (1) GW has not resolved the moral weight for safe abortion provision (staff estimates in 2017 ranged from 1/30 to 1/1 of an infant death averted); (2) no program-level evidence on additionality (whether programs increase safe abortions vs. displacing other safe access); (3) mortality channel is narrow (unsafe abortion CFR ~0.3-0.5% even in SSA — morbidity at 33% complication rate is the main benefit but GW weights mortality more heavily). CE could plausibly clear 8x in favorable scenarios (low-cost SSA settings with high additionality) but depends on methodological choices GW hasn't made. Massive RFMF (~25M unsafe abortions/year globally). Not worth revisiting unless GW resolves its moral weight framework for safe abortion.

Intervention name: Case finding and early treatment for leprosy
Original reason deprioritized: Limited evidence on benefits of early detection; most BOTEC parameters were guesses; small global DALY burden (~30k DALYs/year); no mortality
Reason: Leprosy remains essentially a morbidity-only disease with near-zero mortality and tiny global DALY burden (~30k DALYs/year). GBD 2021 shows continued decline (ASR 0.58→0.25/100k). Key new development is WHO-recommended SDR-PEP (single-dose rifampicin post-exposure prophylaxis): the PEOPLE cluster-RCT (Lancet Global Health 2024) found individual-level IRR 0.55 (p=0.005) and the LPEP programme demonstrated feasibility in 7 countries (150k+ contacts treated). But SDR-PEP shifts the intervention from case detection to prevention — and even optimistic SDR-PEP scenarios yield only ~1–3x cash transfers because there are no deaths to avert and disability weights are low (0.152 for severe impairment). LepVax (first leprosy-specific vaccine) entered Phase 1b in Brazil (Oct 2024) but is too early to affect CE. Novartis continues donating MDT drugs. Original BOTEC had #REF! errors and most parameters remain unresolvable guesses. Not worth revisiting — fundamental CE arithmetic unchanged.

Original reason deprioritized: Costs highly uncertain; moral weight for averting LBW (3 UoV) was a rough guess; WHO recommended MMS only "in context of rigorous research"
Reason: WHO included UNIMMAP MMS on Essential Medicines List (2021) and upgraded recommendation to 'in context of rigorous research' (2020). Kirk Humanitarian committed $125M and reached 75M pregnant women in 111 LMICs at $0.0118/dose ($2.13/pregnancy). 2025 meta-analyses confirm benefits extend beyond birth: stunting reduced (RR 0.86) and underweight reduced (RR 0.86) through 24 months. Copenhagen Consensus 2023 named MMS one of 12 best investments ($37 return per $1). CE improvement driven primarily by reduced commodity costs ($3/pregnancy vs original $4). Key uncertainties: incremental programmatic costs of IFA→MMS transition and MW for LBW (3 UoV is still a rough guess). See writeup.

Original reason deprioritized: Not cost-effective (~2-3x cash); ~$41/beneficiary/year cost is ~83x higher than deworming; uncertain long-term effects of weight gain on adult income
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~2-3x (no material change; possibly higher with long-term income evidence but offset by higher costs and reduced RFMF)
Reason: 2025 Cochrane review (40 studies 91885 students) confirms 'modest but real' effects on weight enrollment and math. New long-term evidence from China (2024) finds +12.4% adult wages from school feeding — but this is from a middle-income country with uncertain external validity to SSA. Sweden study (2022) finds +3% lifetime income. However costs appear higher than the $41/beneficiary the original assumed: CGDev 2023 survey finds $110/child/year in low-income countries for 200 meal days. Space is massively government-funded ($84B/year 99% from domestic budgets). 107 countries now have national school meals policies (up from 56 in 2020). 466M children globally receive school meals. GiveWell has not publicly investigated school feeding. Even if long-term income effects are larger than originally modeled the higher costs and near-zero marginal philanthropic impact (given dominant government funding) make this unpromising for GW. Room for more funding is minimal.

Original reason deprioritized: Small effect size (~1.2 mmHg systolic BP reduction); entire population needs continuous treatment; effect modeled indirectly via BP-to-outcome relationship
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~2-4x (updated with SSaSS hard endpoint data but offset by lower moral weight for elderly CVD deaths)
Reason: The SSaSS trial (NEJM 2021 n=20995 600 villages rural China) found 14% stroke reduction (RR 0.86) 13% MACE reduction (RR 0.87) and 12% all-cause mortality reduction (RR 0.88) — hard clinical endpoints far exceeding what the original BP-based indirect modeling predicted. WHO published first-ever guideline recommending salt substitution (Jan 2025 conditional recommendation). 2024 meta-analysis (Annals of Internal Medicine 16 RCTs 27710 participants): all-cause mortality RR 0.88. SSaSS within-trial CEA found salt substitution was DOMINANT (cost-saving) from health system perspective. Indonesia modeling: cost-saving with 200k+ deaths averted over 10 years. Despite these impressive results CE by GW standards likely only rises to ~2-4x because: (1) CVD deaths are mainly in elderly — the original BOTEC used MW=54.8 but didn't age-adjust and most beneficiaries are 60+ with lower remaining life years; (2) SSaSS population was high-risk (73% prior stroke 88% hypertensive) — general population effects are smaller; (3) Bloomberg/Resolve to Save Lives spending $215M on sodium reduction already. Most evidence from China/Taiwan; generalizability to SSA limited. No organization implementing community salt substitution at scale in LMICs outside trial settings.

Intervention name: WASH interventions to decrease hospital acquired infections
Original CE estimate (x cash): Up to 4x best guess (8-11x under optimistic cost assumptions)
Original reason deprioritized: Weak evidence (no impact evaluation of BASICS or closely related interventions); high complexity (behavioral intervention requiring sustained supply of cleaning products); uncertain costs per birth
Updated rough CE estimate (x cash): ~4x (unchanged; APT-Sepsis trial is encouraging but covers broader intervention than WASH alone)
Reason: APT-Sepsis trial (NEJM 2025 59 facilities Malawi/Uganda 431394 births) found 32% reduction in severe maternal infection outcomes (RR 0.68) — the strongest LMIC evidence to date. However APT-Sepsis is a broad IPC intervention (hand hygiene + antibiotics + sepsis detection + treatment bundle) not just WASH/cleaning. Zambia neonatal IPC bundle (pre-post) found hospital mortality fell from 23.6% to 18.0%. WHO 2024 IPC report estimates 3.5M patients could die from HAIs annually; IPC could avert 821k deaths by 2050. WaterAid 2024: 2.6M HAIs and 275k deaths in 7 SSA countries costing $8.4B/year. But BASICS still has no published impact evaluation — the original QEA's central concern persists. WASH FIT (main WHO tool) implemented in 53+ countries with billions invested but has no rigorous evaluation. Systematic review of neonatal HAI prevention found no studies in low-income countries. Cost per facility ($25k setup + $10k/year) not updated. Policy momentum strong (UN GA resolution 2023; 101 countries reporting) but no clear philanthropic funding opportunity. Even applying the APT-Sepsis effect size (32% vs original 16%) to the BOTEC only raises CE to ~7.6x in high-NNM countries — still below 8x bar.

Intervention name: Digital tools to enhance take-up of welfare programs
Original reason deprioritized: One flawed RCT (Blanco & Vargas 2014 85% attrition self-reported outcomes); 60% IV discount; speculative cost assumptions
Reason: DellaVigna & Linos (2022 Econometrica) found academic nudge effects are 6x larger than at-scale effects (8.7pp vs 1.4pp across 126 RCTs and 23M individuals) — strongly suggesting the original 12pp effect from Blanco & Vargas would not replicate. Hanna et al. (2024 REStat Thailand) found low enrollment may be due to low valuation rather than information barriers — a fundamental challenge for SMS-based approaches. Page et al. (2023) found zero impact from national-scale FAFSA SMS nudges (800k students). J-PAL/CID 2024 evidence review: information alone insufficient; enrollment assistance needed. No new direct RCT on SMS for welfare enrollment in LMICs since Blanco & Vargas 2014. SMS costs very low (<$0.01/message in most LMICs) but effect size deflation more than offsets cost savings. G2P digital infrastructure expanded massively (865M new accounts during COVID) but this benefits direct digital delivery not SMS nudges. CE likely lower than original 3x given scale-up discount on effect size.

Original reason deprioritized: Very limited experimental evidence; Björkman Nyqvist 2022 RCT null (only 10% received kits); cost ~$10/kit; targets only ~11% of neonatal deaths
Reason: Still no well-powered RCT showing mortality reduction from birth kits alone. Arowosegbe et al. 2023 (Nigeria cluster RCT n=453): neonatal infection RR 0.64 (p=0.10) — promising direction but underpowered. iNCK trial in Pakistan (~27000 women) could be pivotal but results pending and tests bundled intervention (kit + CHX + emollient + thermal care). Gebeyehu et al. 2024 meta-analysis of 12 observational studies: CDK use associated with reduced mortality/sepsis but all observational evidence. Lee Him et al. 2024 descriptive review: ~27% mortality reduction from clean birth kits across heterogeneous designs. Cost slightly favorable: JANMA kit ~$2.20 locally assembled (vs ~$10 original); iNCK CEA found $74/DALY averted. No new Cochrane review on birth kits. WHO position unchanged. Global trend toward facility-based care may shrink target population. Even at lower cost ($2.20/kit) CE arithmetic doesn't clear 8x given the small share of neonatal deaths targetable (~11% infection/sepsis-related) and uncertain effectiveness.
